questions.passage:
A public clock once did more than announce the hour. Mounted on a station wall, church tower, factory gate, or school corridor, it made time visible as a common condition. People arranged themselves around it. Trains departed, bells rang, shifts began, and lateness became a social fact rather than a private feeling. The public clock did not merely measure time; it helped produce a community of simultaneous obligation.

Personal devices have not made clocks disappear, but they have changed the experience of being timed. The hour now glows from pockets and wrists, adjusted to individual calendars, alarms, sleep cycles, and notifications. This is convenient and often humane. A nurse working nights, a parent coordinating childcare, or a student managing medication may need time to become personal in order for life to remain possible. The older public clock could be a tyrant, especially when factory whistles translated time into discipline.

But the privatization of time has losses that convenience does not fully register. When everyone consults a private device, the shared question "What time is it?" becomes rarer. More importantly, the answer is less likely to gather people into the same temporal frame. A station clock tells waiting passengers not only the hour but the fact that they are waiting together. A classroom clock, however resented, makes the last five minutes collective. A phone alarm can organize the individual without acknowledging the group.

This matters because shared time is a precondition for certain kinds of public life. Meetings, protests, performances, and rituals all depend on people entering a common duration. The clock above the square does not create solidarity, but it gives solidarity a schedule. Without some common time, coordination becomes a matter of private alerts and negotiated exceptions. We may become more flexible and less punctual, more responsive and less mutually aware.

Nostalgia for public clocks would be misplaced. Standardized time itself arrived through railroads, empires, and factories; it often served power before it served fellowship. The village bell could gather worshippers, but it could also enforce conformity. The question is not whether shared time was innocent. It was not. The question is whether a society can preserve the civic benefits of common timing without restoring its coercions.

Perhaps the public clock's value lies in its impersonality. It does not know whose meeting matters more, whose lateness has an excuse, whose private schedule is full. This indifference can be harsh, but it can also be equalizing. To look up at the same clock is to accept, briefly, that one's private urgency is not the only measure in the room.

The disappearing public clock therefore marks not only a technological shift, but a subtle change in how people notice one another as participants in the same interval.
questions.stem: Which statement best captures the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Personal devices have made people less punctual and therefore less capable of public life.
choices.B: Standardized time was primarily a tool of oppression and should be replaced by flexible scheduling.
choices.C: Public clocks created shared time with civic value and coercive risks.
choices.D: Public clocks are valuable because they measure time more accurately than private devices.

explanations.A: The author mentions possible losses in mutual awareness, but does not claim people are simply less punctual or incapable of public life.
explanations.B: The passage criticizes coercive histories but also emphasizes civic benefits such as meetings, protests, and rituals.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage balances benefits and dangers: public clocks made time common, but standardized time also served factories, empires, and conformity. The author wants common timing without coercion.
explanations.D: Accuracy is not the issue; the concern is shared visibility and social coordination.

questions.stem: The examples of the nurse, parent, and student serve to

choices.A: show that public clocks are obsolete for people with serious responsibilities.
choices.B: acknowledge that personalized time can be necessary and humane.
choices.C: argue that only vulnerable groups benefit from private scheduling devices.
choices.D: prove that shared time was always more oppressive than useful.

explanations.A: The examples justify personal time in some cases, but not the conclusion that public clocks are obsolete.
explanations.B: Correct. These examples qualify the critique of privatized time. They show that individual calendars and alarms can support real needs, so the passage is not nostalgic rejection of personal devices.
explanations.C: The passage lists specific cases to illustrate humane convenience, not to limit benefits to those groups.
explanations.D: The author says shared time has coercive histories and civic benefits, not that oppression always dominates.

questions.stem: What does the author mean by saying a station clock tells passengers 'that they are waiting together'?

choices.A: It encourages passengers to speak with one another about the train schedule.
choices.B: It makes delay visible as a shared condition.
choices.C: It proves that public transportation depends on stricter discipline than other institutions.
choices.D: It turns waiting into a ritual that passengers consciously value.

explanations.A: Conversation may occur, but the passage emphasizes shared awareness rather than speech.
explanations.B: Correct. The point is not conversation but shared temporal framing. The clock makes waiting a common fact experienced in public, not merely a series of private phone checks.
explanations.C: The example is about common timing, not a ranking of institutional discipline.
explanations.D: The author does not claim passengers consciously value waiting as ritual.

questions.stem: The author's discussion of railroads, empires, and factories primarily functions to

choices.A: explain why public clocks are technologically inferior to personal devices.
choices.B: argue that modern societies should reject standardized time altogether.
choices.C: show that religious timekeeping was more democratic than industrial timekeeping.
choices.D: prevent a romantic view of shared time by acknowledging its coercive history.

explanations.A: The issue is social history, not technological inferiority.
explanations.B: The author wants to preserve civic benefits without coercion, not reject standardization completely.
explanations.C: The village bell is also said to enforce conformity, so religion is not idealized.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph explicitly says nostalgia would be misplaced because standardized time often served power. It complicates, rather than cancels, the defense of common timing.

questions.stem: Which new situation would best illustrate the civic benefit the author associates with shared time?

choices.A: A freelancer uses multiple alarms to manage work for clients in different time zones.
choices.B: A commuter privately checks an app that predicts a bus delay to the minute.
choices.C: A factory posts penalties for employees who clock in after a whistle.
choices.D: Residents meet at an announced hour to discuss flooding.

explanations.A: The freelancer example illustrates useful personalization, not shared civic timing.
explanations.B: The bus app gives precise private information, but does not create common temporal awareness.
explanations.C: The factory penalty illustrates the coercive side of standardized time, not its civic benefit.
explanations.D: Correct. The meeting depends on people entering a common duration for public coordination. It matches the passage's claim that solidarity needs a schedule.

questions.stem: The final paragraph suggests that the impersonality of public time can be

choices.A: morally neutral because timekeeping has no political significance.
choices.B: valuable only when individual circumstances are ignored completely.
choices.D: dangerous because it makes all private urgency illegitimate.

explanations.A: The entire passage treats timekeeping as politically and socially significant.
explanations.B: The author acknowledges private needs earlier; equalizing does not require total disregard of circumstance.
explanations.C: Correct. The author says indifference can be harsh but also equalizing. Looking at the same clock briefly limits the assumption that private urgency is the only measure.
explanations.D: Private urgency is not illegitimate; it is simply not the only measure.

questions.stem: Which assumption underlies the passage's defense of public clocks?

choices.A: collective action requires shared temporal awareness.
choices.B: People are more ethical when they rely on public objects rather than private devices.
choices.C: Technological convenience usually weakens social responsibility.
choices.D: Impersonal rules are always fairer than individualized accommodations.

explanations.A: Correct. The argument about meetings, protests, performances, and rituals depends on shared duration being necessary for certain public actions. Public clocks symbolize and support that recognition.
explanations.B: The passage does not make a broad moral contrast between public objects and private devices.
explanations.C: Convenience can be humane; the critique is specific to losses in mutual awareness.
explanations.D: The passage acknowledges that impersonal time can be harsh and that personal accommodations may be necessary.
